userNote: BUG: _handleTickets logs activity with `target_fingerprint` (snake_case) at line 1558, but persistence.logActivity() reads `activity.targetFingerprint` (camelCase). The fingerprint never lands in the DB column for TICKET_CREATED activity rows — it is silently nulled. Fix: rename the key to `targetFingerprint`. Compare with main.js:376 which uses the correct camelCase form.

STALE DOC: _handleRecordCommit's JSDoc block (lines 1434-1446) still claims it fires HOOKS.LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION. Batch 025 changed the implementation to fire HOOKS.COMMIT_RECORDED but the docstring was not updated. Update the JSDoc to match the code.

DEAD CODE: `HOOKS.TICKET_CREATED || 'ticket:created'` at line 1569 — the constant has been in the canonical HOOKS map since the hook was added. The string fallback can never fire. Safe to simplify to `HOOKS.TICKET_CREATED`.

FC4 has zero test coverage. The /api/ prefix skip (line 131) plus the src/ + backend/ + lib/ skips (line 132) were a manual fix in batch 025 caught only by smoke. Add a unit test exercising: (a) a matching gap-report path that exists in file_registry (pass), (b) an /api/* URL (skipped → pass), (c) a real broken path not in file_registry (fail). Today a regression here will silently turn FC4 into a false-negative generator.

FC4 prefix-skip trades false positives for false negatives. Skipping any `src/...` or `backend/...` ref means real broken refs in the gap report under those prefixes are NEVER flagged. The skip was added to suppress hardcoded mapping-table refs in gap-analyzer; a more surgical approach is to pass a known-allowlist into the check rather than prefix-skip wholesale.

Four hook constants have zero firers in the codebase: FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE, FILE_AFTER_CHANGE, LIFECYCLE_TRANSITION, PRD_GENERATE. They are documented extension points but the watcher (main.js onFileChange) still does its work inline and never emits FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE / FILE_AFTER_CHANGE. Either wire the watcher into the registry (batch 023 deferred this as 'a bigger, riskier refactor') or annotate the constants as reserved-but-unfired so contributors know.

The file watcher's onFileChange handler (lines 209+, ~215 lines) is the largest remaining inline orchestration block in main.js. Batch 023's hook conversion explicitly skipped it. It is the natural publisher for FILE_BEFORE_CHANGE / FILE_AFTER_CHANGE. Until it's converted, the registry is the publisher for indexer events only, not watcher events — a structural inconsistency.

PERF: _handleRecordCommit and _handleTickets each construct a fresh St8Persistence and call initialize() (which opens better-sqlite3, applies pragmas, runs the full ST8_SCHEMA exec) on every request. Heavy per-request cost just to fire a hook. The rest of app.js also has 20+ sites doing the same thing — consider a shared `app.persistence` initialized once at server start.

ST8_PORT default is hardcoded to 3847. If the user runs st8 on a non-default port, the shell hook needs ST8_PORT in its env — and git invokes hooks with a minimal env, so the user has to globally export it. Consider reading the port from .st8/config or a .st8/port file written by the server on startup, so the hook auto-targets the actual running instance.

The escape_json sed pipeline only handles backslash, double-quote, tab, CR, and LF. A commit subject containing a literal control character (0x00-0x1F other than \t/\r/\n) will produce invalid JSON and curl will fail silently. Either widen the escaping (use jq if available, fall back to sed) or strip control chars before substitution.

SUBJECT is truncated at 200 bytes via `head -c 200`. If the cut lands inside a multi-byte UTF-8 codepoint, the resulting string has a malformed trailing byte that will break the JSON parse. Either truncate by codepoint (awk/perl) or post-validate the truncation.

Installer overwrites .git/hooks/post-commit unconditionally with `ln -sf`. If a user already has a custom post-commit (e.g. a chained hook runner, husky, lefthook), it is silently clobbered. Detect a pre-existing non-symlink and either chain it or warn loudly.

registerDefaultSubscribers is not idempotent. Calling twice registers each subscriber twice and every INDEX_COMPLETE pass fires the chain twice. The current bootstrap calls it once, but there is no guard. Consider tagging the registry (e.g. registry._defaultsRegistered = true) and short-circuiting on re-entry.

The manifest-generator subscriber (P=10) has no internal try/catch. It relies on the registry-level catch. The gap-analyzer and intent-seeder subscribers DO have internal try/catch. Inconsistent — either all four should be wrapped (and emit module-specific error messages) or none should be. Today a manifest-generator throw produces a generic `[hooks] "index:complete" subscriber "manifest-generator" threw:` line with no context about what failed.

The sonic-daemon INDEX_START subscriber lazy-requires '../../features/search/sonic-daemon' inside the handler. That path is a relative require from default-subscribers.js — if the file is moved, the require path breaks at run time, not load time. Consider hoisting the require to module top (with a try/catch around require itself) so missing-module errors surface during bootstrap.

execute() re-emits via EventEmitter (line 137) AFTER all priority-ordered handlers complete. Throws from .on() listeners are NOT caught by the registry's try/catch — they surface as uncaught exceptions. Either drop the EventEmitter re-emit (no callers use it) or wrap the emit() in its own try/catch with per-listener isolation. The current `try { this.emit(...) } catch (_) {}` only catches synchronous throws from EventEmitter's internal dispatch, not from individual listeners.

No tests. The repo has zero *.test.js files under src/. Hook registry semantics (priority ordering, async sequencing, error isolation, unregister returns) are all on the honor system. Minimum viable test suite: priority order, async await sequencing, one handler throwing does not block the next, unregister actually unregisters, listHooks() introspection. ~50 lines of tape/jest.

FC3 fails by design when file_registry accumulates rows from prior runs (batch 025 documented this). Batch 026 added pruneFilesNotIn() to fix the symptom. If pruning is ever removed or fails, FC3 will start failing without an obvious link back to the cleanup logic. Add a hint in FC3's failure message pointing at pruneFilesNotIn / Pass-0 prune.

FC5 silently skips when persistence.getAllConnections() is not implemented (line 148). The check returns ok:true with message 'skipped'. That is a YELLOW signal masquerading as GREEN. Either return ok:false with a non-blocking severity tag, or surface 'check skipped' in the report's summary line, so a missing method does not look like a passing test.
runForceChecks writes its report with synchronous fs.writeFileSync inside an async handler. On a large project this is a brief stop-the-world. Switch to fs.promises.writeFile.

_handleRecordCommit's hook fire is non-fatal (catches and logs). _handleTickets's hook fire is also non-fatal. Good. But the lazy require inside the catch happens AFTER the persistence write — if the require itself throws (unlikely but possible during a circular-require shakeout), the ticket/commit is already in the DB while the hook never fires. Subscribers expect at-least-once delivery for these events; in practice they get at-most-once. Document or add a retry queue.

TICKET_CREATED hook payload spreads the full request payload: `{ ticket: { id, ...payload, createdAt } }`. The payload may include unexpected client-supplied fields. Lock the shape down: explicitly enumerate { id, fingerprint, filepath, userNote, sha256Hash, statusAtCreation, identityBundle, createdAt } so subscribers can rely on a stable contract.

_handleTicketsCount and _handleTickets GET path do not fire any hook. Fine today, but if a future subscriber needs 'someone read tickets' (e.g. to mark a phreak> session as 'aware'), there is no observable event. Consider TICKET_READ as a future extension point.

listHooks() returns only registered hooks — hooks declared in HOOKS but with zero subscribers are absent from the introspection output. A debugging tool would want to see the full canonical map plus subscriber counts (including zero). Add a HOOKS export passthrough or a listAllHooks() that joins HOOKS keys with registered counts.

There is no way to introspect the order in which subscribers will run from outside the registry. listHooks() returns sources unsorted within a hook entry (it preserves registration order, not priority order). Consumers reading listHooks output cannot tell at a glance which subscriber goes first. Sort the inner sources by priority before returning.

Hook fires (INDEX_START, FILE_INDEXED, INDEX_COMPLETE) await sequentially inside the bootstrap path. FILE_INDEXED fires per-file inside a for loop with await; for a 1000-file project that is 1000 sequential awaits even if no subscribers are registered. The per-iteration overhead is small but real (Promise allocation + microtask flush). Consider skipping execute() when subscriber count is zero, or batching.

All four INDEX_COMPLETE subscribers swallow errors and continue. A failure in manifest-generator (P=10) does NOT prevent schema-card-emitter (P=20) from running, even though emitter depends on the manifest's outputs in the gap-analyzer/force-check chain. This is per-design isolation, but the consequence is a 'partial run' where downstream subscribers operate on stale or missing inputs. Consider a fail-fast mode for development.

POST /api/record-commit and POST /api/tickets have no authentication. The post-commit shell hook posts from localhost, but the server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default in some configs. A remote attacker could pollute activity_log with arbitrary COMMIT_RECORDED entries or create fake tickets. Bind to 127.0.0.1 by default, or add an origin/host check on these write routes.

POST /api/record-commit accepts arbitrary `filesChanged` value type — the route does not validate that it's a number. The shell hook always produces a number, but anyone POSTing manually could pass a string/object/array and it lands in activity_log.details as-is. Tighten validation to {hash: string, filesChanged?: number, ...}.
